title: CC104: Data Structures & Algorithms — Overview
description: A big-picture look at what data structures and algorithms are, the common types you'll meet, and why they matter
congratsText: Great work! You now have a solid overview of data structures and algorithms and how they fit together.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Define data structure and algorithm, and explain how they work together
  • Name and describe common data structures: arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues
  • Name and describe common algorithm types: searching and sorting
  • Explain, informally, why some solutions are 'better' than others (efficiency)
  • Explain why this course builds its foundation on arrays

sections.title: What is a Data Structure?

quiz.question: Which best describes a data structure?
options: A set of steps a program follows to solve a problem
An organized way to store and arrange data so it can be used efficiently
A type of error message shown by the compiler
A function that only prints text to the screen

sections.lesson:
What is a Data Structure?
A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data so it can be accessed and used efficiently — like a filing cabinet, a row of numbered lockers, or a stack of trays. The right structure makes the data easy to work with.

sections.title: What is an Algorithm?

quiz.question: Which best describes an algorithm?
options: A container that holds values in memory
A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or completing a task
A keyword used to declare a variable
A synonym for 'data structure'

sections.lesson:
What is an Algorithm?
An algorithm is a clear, ordered set of steps for solving a problem — like the instructions for finding a name in a contact list, or sorting a stack of papers by date.

sections.title: How They Work Together

quiz.question: In the pair 'contact list + searching a name,' which part is the data structure and which is the algorithm?
options: Contact list = algorithm; searching a name = data structure
Contact list = data structure; searching a name = algorithm
Both are data structures — searching isn't an algorithm
Both are algorithms — a contact list is just a list of steps

sections.lesson:
How They Work Together
Data structures and algorithms are a team: the structure is the box, the algorithm is the steps to use the box. A contact list (structure) is only useful once you can search it (algorithm).

sections.title: Common Data Structures: Array

quiz.question: What makes an array a useful data structure?
options: It stores one single value with no way to access it by position
It stores a fixed row of values, each reachable instantly by an index number
It only works for text, never for numbers
It automatically sorts itself whenever a value is added

sections.lesson:
Common Data Structures: Array
An array is a row of numbered 'lockers' — a fixed set of values you can reach instantly using an index (starting at 0). Arrays are the foundation of this course.

sections.title: Common Data Structures: Linked List, Stack & Queue

quiz.question: A cafeteria tray stack, where the last tray placed is the first one taken, is an example of which structure?
options: Queue (FIFO — first in, first out)
Array (fixed, indexed row)
Stack (LIFO — last in, first out)
Linked list (a chain of connected pieces)

sections.lesson:
Common Data Structures: Linked List, Stack & Queue
Beyond arrays, other structures solve different problems:
  • Linked list — a chain of pieces, each pointing to the next, like a human chain holding hands
  • Stack (LIFO) — last in, first out, like a stack of cafeteria trays or the 'undo' button
  • Queue (FIFO) — first in, first out, like a store line or a printer's job queue

sections.title: Common Algorithms: Searching

quiz.question: What does a search algorithm do?
options: It rearranges data into a specific order
It looks through data to find whether (and where) a target value exists
It permanently deletes unused variables
It only works on data structures with fewer than 5 elements

sections.lesson:
Common Algorithms: Searching
A search algorithm finds whether a value exists in a structure, and where. The simplest form, linear search, checks each item in order until it finds a match (or runs out of items).

sections.title: Common Algorithms: Sorting

quiz.question: Why would a program sort its data before searching it?
options: Sorting has no effect on how searching works
A sorted arrangement can make searching faster or allow it to stop early
Sorting deletes duplicate values automatically
Sorting is only useful for printing data, not searching it

sections.lesson:
Common Algorithms: Sorting
A sort algorithm puts data in order (ascending or descending). Once sorted, other tasks — like searching — can often be done faster or more simply, since you can stop looking once you've passed where a value should be.

sections.title: Why Efficiency Matters

quiz.question: Two search methods both find the right answer, but one checks every item while the other skips half the remaining items each time. Why does this difference matter?
options: It doesn't — both are always equally fast in practice
The method that skips more items is generally more efficient on large data
Efficiency only matters for algorithms that produce wrong answers
The one that checks every item is always faster

sections.lesson:
Why Efficiency Matters
Different algorithms can solve the same problem with very different amounts of work. Counting comparisons (like in linear search) is an early, intuitive way to notice this — it's the seed of what's formally called Big-O efficiency, covered later in the course.

sections.title: Why This Course Builds on Arrays

quiz.question: Why does CC104's midterm focus almost entirely on arrays rather than linked lists, stacks, and queues?
options: Because arrays are the only real data structure in computer science
Because arrays are simpler for beginners, avoid pointer-chasing, and still solve most beginner-level problems
Because linked lists, stacks, and queues cannot be implemented in C
Because arrays are unrelated to algorithms like searching and sorting

sections.lesson:
Why This Course Builds on Arrays
Arrays give beginners a simple, fixed structure to practice core ideas — traversal, searching, sorting — without the added complexity of pointer-chasing. Other structures (linked lists, stacks, queues) are introduced by name and analogy, so you recognize them, before deeper study later.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: Which statement best summarizes this overview?
options: Data structures organize data, algorithms act on it, and different structures/algorithms trade off simplicity and efficiency — this course starts with arrays
Data structures and algorithms are two names for the same idea
Only sorting counts as an algorithm; searching is a data structure
Efficiency is not something beginner programmers need to think about

sections.lesson:
Recap
Data structures organize information (arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues); algorithms act on that information (searching, sorting). Different choices trade off simplicity and efficiency. This course starts with arrays because they're the clearest foundation for learning these ideas in C.
